title: Host Discovery
summary: Enumerate live hosts on a network segment — run this when you land on a pivot host and discover a new subnet.

title: Nmap / RustScan
summary: Fast port discovery followed by targeted service scanning — the first thing you run on every target.

title: Passive Recon / OSINT
summary: Gather target intelligence without touching the target — subdomains, emails, ASN ranges, and breach data.

title: DNS Enumeration
summary: Extract subdomains and internal hostnames via zone transfer and brute force — expands the attack surface significantly.

title: FTP Enumeration
summary: Check for anonymous access, enumerate files, and identify version-specific exploits on FTP services.

title: IPMI Enumeration
summary: Exploit IPMI 2.0 to dump password hashes via RAKP authentication or bypass auth entirely with Cipher 0 — cracked hashes are frequently reused for SSH and web admin panels.

title: Kerberos User Enumeration
summary: Confirm valid domain usernames using KDC error code differences — no credentials required, and valid users can be immediately tested for AS-REP roastability.

title: LDAP Enumeration
summary: Extract the full AD object tree — users, groups, descriptions, and pre-auth flags — often without credentials.

title: MSSQL Enumeration
summary: Connect to MSSQL, escalate to sysadmin via impersonation, and execute OS commands — SQL Server service accounts almost always have SeImpersonatePrivilege.

title: MySQL Enumeration
summary: Connect to MySQL, read sensitive files, and write web shells — root with no password is common in lab environments.

title: NFS Enumeration
summary: List and mount NFS exports — no_root_squash and UID spoofing are both direct paths to reading restricted files or planting SUID binaries.

title: PostgreSQL Enumeration
summary: Connect to PostgreSQL and escalate to OS command execution via COPY TO PROGRAM — superuser access (common on default installs) is all it takes.

title: RDP Enumeration
summary: Check NLA status, encryption level, and vulnerability to BlueKeep/DejaBlue — NLA disabled means password brute-force is viable.

title: Redis Enumeration
summary: Unauthenticated Redis running as root can have an SSH key written directly into authorized_keys — instant foothold.

title: Rsync Enumeration
summary: List and download Rsync modules without authentication — home directories exposed here mean SSH key injection is minutes away.

title: SMB Enumeration
summary: Enumerate shares, users, and vulnerabilities over SMB — the most common Windows foothold service.

title: SMTP Enumeration
summary: Enumerate valid usernames via VRFY/RCPT commands and check for open relay misconfigurations.

title: SNMP Enumeration
summary: Read running processes, users, installed software, and open ports from SNMP — often unauthenticated with default community string 'public'.

title: SSH Enumeration
summary: Banner grab for version-based exploits, check auth methods to know if password brute-force is viable, and test default credentials before running full wordlists.

title: VNC Enumeration
summary: Check for null authentication or weak single-password VNC access — GUI access gives clipboard, file transfer, and full desktop without a traditional shell.

title: Web Enumeration
summary: Identify web stack, discover hidden paths, and find attack surface before touching any functionality.

title: AS-REP Roasting
summary: Request AS-REP hashes for accounts that don't require Kerberos pre-authentication — no credentials needed, just a username list.

title: Command Injection
summary: Inject OS commands into a web application that passes user input to a shell — distinct from SQLi and SSTI, with its own bypass patterns for spaces, special characters, and WAFs.

title: File Upload → Shell
summary: Bypass file upload restrictions to plant a web shell — extension filters, content-type checks, and magic bytes are the three layers to defeat.

title: JWT Attacks
summary: Forge or manipulate JSON Web Tokens to escalate privileges or impersonate other users — algorithm confusion and weak secrets are the two most common paths.

title: Kerberoasting
summary: Request TGS tickets for service accounts with SPNs and crack them offline — service accounts often have weak passwords set years ago and never rotated.

title: LFI → RCE
summary: Read arbitrary files through a vulnerable include parameter — then escalate to RCE by poisoning log files or abusing PHP wrappers.

title: NTLM Relay
summary: Intercept and relay NTLM authentication to a target you control — SMB signing disabled is the prerequisite, and relaying to ADCS can yield a domain admin certificate.

title: SMB Null Session
summary: Access SMB shares and RPC endpoints without any credentials — leaks users, groups, password policies, and SYSVOL contents.

title: Password Cracking
summary: Crack captured hashes offline with hashcat or john — NTLMv2 from Responder, AS-REP and TGS blobs from Kerberos attacks, and /etc/shadow from file read all land here before becoming usable credentials.

title: Password Spraying
summary: Test one password against all known users — the safest way to turn a username list into a credential without triggering lockouts.

title: Print Spooler / Coercion Attacks
summary: Force a privileged machine (domain controller, exchange server) to authenticate to an attacker-controlled host — feeds directly into NTLM relay or unconstrained delegation TGT harvesting.

title: PrintNightmare (CVE-2021-1675 / 34527)
summary: Exploit Windows Print Spooler RCE to load a malicious DLL as SYSTEM — works on unpatched DCs and workstations with valid credentials.

title: Public Exploit (CVE)
summary: Match service version banners against known CVEs — many OSCP/CPTS targets are intentionally running unpatched software with public PoCs.

title: Responder / LLMNR Poisoning
summary: Poison LLMNR/NBT-NS queries to capture NTLMv2 hashes or relay them to unsigned SMB targets — run immediately on joining any Windows network.

title: SQLi → RCE
summary: Exploit SQL injection to read credentials, write web shells, or execute OS commands — the path from SQLi to shell depends on the database engine.

title: SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery)
summary: Force the server to make HTTP requests on your behalf — reach internal services, cloud metadata endpoints, and unexposed admin panels that aren't directly reachable from your IP.

title: SSTI → RCE
summary: Inject template syntax into user-controlled fields to break out of the template context and execute arbitrary OS commands.

title: XSS / CSRF
summary: Steal admin session cookies via reflected or stored XSS, or forge authenticated requests via CSRF — used to pivot from a low-priv web account to an admin context with additional attack surface.

title: XXE Injection
summary: Inject a malicious XML entity to read local files or trigger SSRF — any endpoint that parses XML without disabling external entities is vulnerable.

title: Pass the Hash / Ticket
summary: Authenticate with an NTLM hash or Kerberos ticket instead of a plaintext password — every hash you dump should be sprayed across the subnet immediately.

title: RDP Access
summary: Connect to Windows via RDP for a full GUI session — supports pass-the-hash and is the best interface for interactive tools like BloodHound and browser-based admin panels.

title: Reverse Shell
summary: Convert code execution into an interactive session by connecting the target back to your listener — the universal handoff from exploit to shell.

title: SMB Lateral Movement
summary: Move laterally using plaintext credentials or hashes over SMB, WMI, or DCOM — choose the tool based on noise level and what ports are open on the target.

title: SSH Access
summary: Authenticate to SSH with a password, private key, or brute-forced credential — the most stable and interactive Linux foothold available.

title: Web Shell
summary: Plant a minimal script on the server to execute OS commands via HTTP — a stepping stone to a full reverse shell, not a final destination.

title: WinRM Access
summary: Get a PowerShell session over WinRM with evil-winrm — supports password and hash auth, and is the fastest path to an interactive Windows shell without needing a GUI.

title: ACL Abuse (AD)
summary: Exploit Active Directory ACL misconfigurations — GenericAll, WriteDACL, ForceChangePassword, and similar edges identified by BloodHound translate directly into account takeover or DCSync.

title: ADCS ESC1 — Enrollee Supplies Subject
summary: Request a certificate as any user (including Administrator) from a template that lets enrollees specify their own Subject Alternative Name — certipy handles the full chain.

title: ADCS ESC4 — Writable Template ACL
summary: Overwrite a certificate template's configuration to make it ESC1-vulnerable, then exploit it as Administrator — requires write access to the template object in AD.

title: ADCS ESC6 — EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2
summary: A CA-level flag that makes every Client Authentication template enrollable with an arbitrary Subject Alternative Name — certipy finds it, and exploitation is identical to ESC1.

title: ADCS ESC8 — NTLM Relay to Web Enrollment
summary: Relay a domain controller's NTLM authentication to the ADCS Web Enrollment endpoint to obtain a DC certificate — then use it for DCSync without needing any DA credentials.

title: BloodHound Enumeration
summary: Map all attack paths from your current account to Domain Admin — run BloodHound before Kerberoasting, before Certipy, before anything else once you have valid creds.

title: DCSync
summary: Replicate password hashes from a domain controller using DRSUAPI — requires DCSync rights (DA, DC, or explicit delegation) and gives you every account hash in the domain.

title: Kerberos Delegation Abuse
summary: Abuse Unconstrained, Constrained, or Resource-Based Constrained Delegation to impersonate privileged users — BloodHound shows all three types as delegation edges.

title: LAPS & gMSA Abuse
summary: Read LAPS local admin passwords or gMSA service account NTLM hashes from AD if your account has ReadProperty access — BloodHound shows both as explicit edges.

title: Linux Credential Hunting
summary: Search config files, shell history, and SSH keys for credentials — database passwords in web configs are frequently reused as system passwords.

title: Linux Cron Abuse
summary: Find cron jobs running as root that use writable scripts or call binaries without full paths — any write access to what root runs is a path to root.

title: Docker Escape
summary: Break out of a Docker container via the socket, privileged mode, or dangerous capabilities — the socket is the most common and gives full daemon control over the host.

title: Linux Kernel Exploits
summary: Exploit unpatched kernel vulnerabilities for direct root — PwnKit (CVE-2021-4034) is the most reliable and works on nearly every Linux system built before January 2022.

title: NFS no_root_squash Privesc
summary: Plant a SUID bash binary via an NFS export with no_root_squash — you create the binary as root on your attacker machine, the target executes it as root with no shell required.

title: Linux PAM / Polkit Privesc
summary: Chain CVE-2025-6018 (fake console session via PAM) with CVE-2025-6019 (udisks XFS resize race) to plant an SUID root binary — specific to openSUSE/SLE15.

title: Linux PATH Hijacking
summary: Plant a malicious binary earlier in PATH than the real one — exploitable when a SUID binary or root cron job calls a program by bare name without a full path.

title: Shared Library Hijacking
summary: Hijack shared library loading via LD_PRELOAD, writable RPATH, or ldconfig paths — a SUID binary or sudo command loading your library runs your code as root.

title: Linux Sudo Abuse
summary: Check what you can run with sudo and abuse it for a root shell — GTFOBins covers 90% of cases, and misconfigurations like LD_PRELOAD or sudo < 1.8.28 cover the rest.

title: Linux SUID / Capabilities
summary: Find binaries with the SUID bit or elevated capabilities — both allow code execution at a higher privilege level without sudo, and GTFOBins covers most cases.

title: Linux Writable File Abuse
summary: Write to privileged files as a low-priv user — /etc/passwd, sudoers, cron.d, or service unit files — to create a root backdoor or execute commands as root without cracking anything.

title: MSSQL Privilege Escalation
summary: Escalate from a SQL login to OS command execution via xp_cmdshell, impersonation chains, or linked server abuse — SQL Server service accounts almost always have SeImpersonatePrivilege.

title: Shadow Credentials (KeyCredentialLink)
summary: Write a certificate credential to a user or computer object's KeyCredentialLink attribute — then authenticate via PKINIT to get that account's NT hash without changing its password.

title: Token Impersonation (SeImpersonate)
summary: Abuse SeImpersonatePrivilege or SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege to impersonate SYSTEM — present on virtually every service account, IIS app pool, and SQL Server account.

title: DLL Hijacking
summary: Plant a malicious DLL in a directory searched before the legitimate one — exploitable when a privileged process loads a DLL from a user-writable path or when a DLL is missing entirely.

title: GPP Credentials (MS14-025)
summary: Decrypt AES-encrypted passwords from Group Policy Preference XML files on SYSVOL — the key is public knowledge, and old entries persist indefinitely despite the 2014 patch.

title: Windows Registry Privesc
summary: Exploit registry misconfigurations — AlwaysInstallElevated installs MSI packages as SYSTEM, writable AutoRun keys persist malicious executables, and unquoted paths in registry service configs execute injected binaries.

title: SeBackupPrivilege
summary: Use SeBackupPrivilege to bypass ACLs and read any file — dump SAM/SYSTEM for local hashes, or dump NTDS.dit from a DC for every domain account hash.

title: Windows Service Misconfiguration
summary: Replace a writable service binary or exploit an unquoted service path to execute code as SYSTEM when the service starts — winPEAS catches all of these automatically.

title: Windows Stored Credentials
summary: Extract plaintext credentials from cmdkey, the registry, PowerShell history, and credential managers — Winlogon autologon entries are almost always plaintext and reused.

title: UAC Bypass
summary: Elevate from medium to high integrity without a UAC prompt by hijacking COM object registry keys — requires being in the local Administrators group already.

title: Domain Admin / Proof
summary: You have Domain Admin — dump all hashes, screenshot proof, grab krbtgt for persistence, and enumerate additional forests before finishing.

title: Domain / Forest Trust Abuse
summary: Abuse Active Directory trust relationships to move from a compromised child domain or forest to parent domains or trusted forests — requires DA in the source domain and the krbtgt hash.

title: Golden Ticket
summary: Forge a TGT using the krbtgt hash for indefinite domain access — survives Administrator password changes and lasts 10 years by default.

title: Pivot / Tunneling
summary: Route traffic through a compromised host to reach additional network segments — Ligolo-ng eliminates proxychains overhead, Chisel works through strict firewalls.

title: Root Flag / Proof (Linux)
summary: You have root — grab the proof file, dump /etc/shadow for password reuse, plant SSH key persistence, and check for additional network segments to pivot into.

title: Silver Ticket
summary: Forge a TGS for a specific service using the service account's NTLM hash — bypasses the KDC entirely and leaves no DC-side logs.

title: SYSTEM Shell / Proof (Windows)
summary: You have SYSTEM — grab proof, dump LSASS and SAM for credentials, spray hashes laterally, and check for additional subnets before finishing.
